texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A NEW CLASS OF CONDITIONAL MARKOV JUMP PROCESSES WITH REGIME SWITCHING AND PATH DEPENDENCE: PROPERTIES AND MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD ESTIMATION
texts.text: A NEW CLASS OF CONDITIONAL MARKOV JUMP PROCESSES WITH REGIME SWITCHING AND PATH DEPENDENCE: PROPERTIES AND MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD ESTIMATION

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: BUDHI SURYA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. This paper develops a new class of conditional Markov jump processes with regime switching and paths dependence. The key novel feature of the developed process lies on its ability to switch the transition rate as it moves from one state to another with switching probability depending on the current state and time of the process as well as its past trajectories. As such, the transition from current state to another depends on the holding time of the process in the state. Distributional properties of the process are given explicitly in terms of the speed regimes represented by a finite number of different transition matrices, the probabilities of selecting regime membership within each state, and past realization of the process. In particular, it has distributional equivalent stochastic representation with a general mixture of Markov jump processes introduced in Frydman and Surya [15]. Maximum likelihood estimates (MLE) of the distribution parameters of the process are derived in closed form. The estimation is done iteratively using the EM algorithm. Akaike information criterion is used to assess the goodness-of-fit of the selected model. An explicit observed Fisher information matrix of the MLE is derived for the calculation of standard errors of the MLE. The information matrix takes on a simplified form of the general matrix formula of Louis [24]. Large sample properties of the MLE are presented. In particular, the covariance matrix for the MLE of transition rates is equal to the Cram´ er-Rao lower bound, and is less for the MLE of regime membership. The simulation study confirms these findings and shows that the parameter estimates are accurate, consistent, and have asymptotic normality as the sample size increases.

In particular, it has distributional equivalent stochastic representation with a general mixture of Markov jump processes introduced in Frydman and Surya [15]. Maximum likelihood estimates (MLE) of the distribution parameters of the process are derived in closed form. The estimation is done iteratively using the EM algorithm. Akaike information criterion is used to assess the goodness-of-fit of the selected model. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Markov jump process has been one of the most important probabilistic model since its introduction by Markov [25] in 1906. See Seneta [33] for history of the creation of the Markov chains. It is a simple parametric model, but yet a rather complex nonparametric model for describing sequence of events where development of a future event depends only on the current state and the duration it takes. The distribution parameter of the process is given in terms of a transition matrix Q whose ( x, y ) -component q xy defines the rates of making a jump from a state x to another state y .
texts.text: Markov jump process has been one of the most important probabilistic model since its introduction by Markov [25] in 1906. See Seneta [33] for history of the creation of the Markov chains. It is a simple parametric model, but yet a rather complex nonparametric model for describing sequence of events where development of a future event depends only on the current state and the duration it takes. The distribution parameter of the process is given in terms of a transition matrix Q whose ( x, y ) -component q xy defines the rates of making a jump from a state x to another state y .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Statistical inference of the transition matrix was discussed in Albert [3]. Distributional properties of the Markov process have been well studied in literature. We refer among others to Norris [27], Pardoux [30], Resnick [32], and Stroock [34]. The Markov process has been widely used in variety of applications across various fields such as, among others, in modeling vegetation dynamics (Balzter [4]); demography (Nowak [28]); in marketing to model consumer relationship (Berger and Nasr [5] and Pfeifer and Carraway [31]), and to identify substitutions behavior of customers in assortment problem (Blanchet et al. [9]); in describing credit rating transitions used in many credit risk and pricing applications (Jarrow and Turnbull [23], Jarrow et al. [22], Bielecki et al. [7], and Bielecki and Rutkowski [8]); in queueing networks and performance engineering (Bolch et al. [11], Pardoux [30]). It has also been widely used for multi-state analysis of life history and panel data in which a Markov jump process is used to describe the progression of health status of a cohort. See for e.g., Aalen and Gjessing [1], and Cook and Lawless [12]. Statistical softwares for the multi-state analysis by de Wreede et al. [40] and Jackson [20] are freely available in an R package which make implementation of the Markov model against life history and panel data possible and convenient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to its lack of memory property, future evolution of the Markov jump process depends only on its current state, but not on its past history. Furthermore, transition from one state to any phase of the state space is only determined by duration of the transition, but not on the current time ('age' of the process). These might suggest a needed extension of the Markov process.
texts.text: Due to its lack of memory property, future evolution of the Markov jump process depends only on its current state, but not on its past history. Furthermore, transition from one state to any phase of the state space is only determined by duration of the transition, but not on the current time ('age' of the process). These might suggest a needed extension of the Markov process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years there have been attempts to extend the Markov model into one which relaxes the memoryless and stationary properties. Bielecki et. al. [6], and Jakubowski and Nieweglowski [21] proposed an F -conditional doubly stochastic Markov chains given natural filtration (past realization of the sample paths) of the Markov jump process and the filtration generated by exogenous covariate defined by a stochastic intensity as such that future development of a state of the process depends on external factor in non-stationary way. However, the constructed process retains the memoryless property and did not address a possible heterogeneity of cohort in its application using life history or panel data. No details on a maximum likelihood estimation of the model was provided. In 1955, Blumen et al. [10] introduced the seminal mover-stayer (MS) model, which is the mixture of two discrete-time Markov chains used to account for heterogeneity in a cohort of workers in labor market. It was the first mixture of Markov chains considered in the literature. For its continuous-time counterpart, the MS model describes a cluster of workers consisting of stayers (workers who always stay in the same job category) and movers (workers who move to other job category according to a Markov process). Frydman [18] discussed maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) of the MS model by direct maximization of the observed likelihood function. The EM algorithm for the estimation of the MS model was proposed by Fuchs and Greenhouse [19].

[10] introduced the seminal mover-stayer (MS) model, which is the mixture of two discrete-time Markov chains used to account for heterogeneity in a cohort of workers in labor market. It was the first mixture of Markov chains considered in the literature. For its continuous-time counterpart, the MS model describes a cluster of workers consisting of stayers (workers who always stay in the same job category) and movers (workers who move to other job category according to a Markov process). Frydman [18] discussed maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) of the MS model by direct maximization of the observed likelihood function. The EM algorithm for the estimation of the MS model was proposed by Fuchs and Greenhouse [19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Frydman [17] extended the MS model [10] into a particular mixture of Markov jump processes. In its implementation using life history data, the model allows each cohort to move with respect to its own Markov process. The Markov mixture model [17] was successfully applied to modeling credit ratings migration by Frydman and Schuermann [16] in which they found that bonds of the same credit rating may move at different speeds to other credit ratings. In addition to that, they observed that the inclusion of past credit ratings improves out-of-sample prediction of NelsonAalen estimate of credit default intensity. The empirical findings in [16] suggest that the credit rating migration could be represented by a mixture of Markov jump processes moving at different speeds. The mixture model [18] was also successfully applied to clustering of categorical time series by Pamminger and Fruhwirth-Schnatter [29]. Surya [36] revisited [16] and gave further explicit distributional identities of the mixture process, in particular in the presence of an absorbing state. Despite each underlying Markov process of the mixture [17] has different exit rates, the respective embedded Markov chains of the underlying Markov processes have the same transition matrix. This constraint on the transition matrix of the embedded Markov chains may obscure clustering of an heterogeneous cohort, which might result in being rejected using the likelihood ratio (LR) test when compared to a general mixture of Markov jump processes. See Frydman and Surya [15] for details of the LR test, MLE of the general mixture, and asymptotic properties of the MLE. And to Surya [35] for the joint probability distributions of absorption times of the mixture process.

See Frydman and Surya [15] for details of the LR test, MLE of the general mixture, and asymptotic properties of the MLE. texts.label: text
texts.orig: By formulating the general mixture process [15] and [35] in terms of its underlying Markov jump processes, a distributional equivalent stochastic representation of the mixture process is derived in terms of a regime switching conditional Markov jump process with path dependence . The governing equations for the dynamics of the conditional Markov jump process (CMJP) are presented in terms of an increasing sequence of epoch times of the process and the states reached at the epoch times.
texts.text: By formulating the general mixture process [15] and [35] in terms of its underlying Markov jump processes, a distributional equivalent stochastic representation of the mixture process is derived in terms of a regime switching conditional Markov jump process with path dependence . The governing equations for the dynamics of the conditional Markov jump process (CMJP) are presented in terms of an increasing sequence of epoch times of the process and the states reached at the epoch times.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 outlines the research contribution of the paper. Representation of the general mixture process [15], [35] and the proposed conditional Markov jump process are discussed in details in this section. Section 3 presents distributional properties of CMJP. Maximum likelihood estimates of the distribution parameters and EM algorithm for the estimation are discussed in Section 4. Also, the observed Fisher information matrix of the estimates is derived in this section. Large sample properties of the MLE are presented in Section 5. To verify the main results presented in Sections 2-5, a series of simulation studies are performed in Section 6. The simulation study shows that the parameter estimates are accurate with their biases and root mean square errors decreasing by the size of generated sample paths of CMJP. Akaike information criterion is employed to test goodness-of-fit of the selected model against the data it was generated from. Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is conducted for each parameter bias. The test is found to be statistically significant at the acceptance level α = 5% for each parameter bias, confirming the consistency and asymptotic normality of the estimates. Section 7 concludes this paper.
texts.text: This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 outlines the research contribution of the paper. Representation of the general mixture process [15], [35] and the proposed conditional Markov jump process are discussed in details in this section. Section 3 presents distributional properties of CMJP. Maximum likelihood estimates of the distribution parameters and EM algorithm for the estimation are discussed in Section 4. Also, the observed Fisher information matrix of the estimates is derived in this section. Large sample properties of the MLE are presented in Section 5. To verify the main results presented in Sections 2-5, a series of simulation studies are performed in Section 6. The simulation study shows that the parameter estimates are accurate with their biases and root mean square errors decreasing by the size of generated sample paths of CMJP. Akaike information criterion is employed to test goodness-of-fit of the selected model against the data it was generated from. Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is conducted for each parameter bias. The test is found to be statistically significant at the acceptance level α = 5% for each parameter bias, confirming the consistency and asymptotic normality of the estimates. Section 7 concludes this paper.

texts.orig: The state diagram of the mixture process X (3) is illustrated in Figure 1. The diagram shows that conditional on the event { Φ = m } , X moves w.r.t the Markov process X ( m ) , i.e., once the speed regime Φ is selected, X evolves according to the selected Markov process. As ∑ M m =1 Φ ( m ) = 1, X defines a finite mixture of Markov processes { X ( m ) : 1 ≤ m ≤ M } . Since the Markov processes { X ( m ) } are defined on the same state space S with the same transition diagram, there is uncertainty concerning which underlying process that drives the dynamics of X . That is, when X is observed in a state x ∈ S , there is one out of M possible underlying processes { X ( m ) } by which X arrived in x . Unlike its underlying processes, X does not have the Markov property. This can be seen from the fact that Φ ( m ) = 1 if and only if X = X ( m ) , i.e., the observed process X is driven by X ( m ) .

texts.orig: Figure 2 exhibits a transition diagram of X (6). The process selects an initial state x 0 ∈ S = { 1 , · · · , p } based on the distribution α before it makes a transition from one phase of the state space S to another. When the process makes a transition from its current state x at time t ≥ 0 to another state, say y , it may switch the transition rate among M possible choices { q xy, 1 , · · · , q xy,M } with switching probability distribution φ x, · ( t ) = ( φ x, 1 ( t ) , · · · , φ x,M ( t ) ) (4) depending on the current state and time of the process as well as its past trajectories. The chosen rate q xy,m determines the underlying Markov process X ( m ) by which X makes the transition. As the process is non Markovian, the transition depends on the holding time of the process in the current state. See Section 3 for details. Algorithm for simulation of sample paths of X is presented in Section 6.2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Maximum likelihood estimation of the distribution parameters
texts.text: 4. Maximum likelihood estimation of the distribution parameters

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section discusses maximum likelihood estimation of distribution parameters of the conditional Markov jump process X (6) based on continuous observation of the sample paths. To be more precise, we are interested in estimating the initial regime switching probability φ x,m and the intensity matrices Q m = [ q xy,m ] xy , for ( x, y ) ∈ S and 1 ≤ m ≤ M from continuously observed realizations of the process. In general, the estimation problem is challenging and quite difficult given that the process changes its speed/underlying process at random times. However, thanks to their distributional equivalence with the mixture of Markov jump processes (3), the estimation can be done systematically using the EM algorithm with an explicit form of parameter estimates. Estimated variance of the parameter estimates are discussed along with their asymptotic properties.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Simulation study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section exemplifies the main results presented in Sections 3 - 5 through a series of simulation studies. In particular, we are interested in applying the EM algorithm to get maximum likelihood estimates ̂ θ 0 of the distribution parameter θ 0 of the process X (6) based on Monte Carlo simulation of its sample paths. Algorithm for simulation of the sample paths is presented. Estimated standard error ̂ Var( ̂ θ 0 ) of the MLE ̂ θ 0 is obtained using the inverse of observed Fisher information matrix J p ( ̂ θ 0 ) and compared with the actual standard errors Var( ̂ θ 0 ). Asymptotic properties (consistency, normality, and efficiency) of the MLE ̂ θ 0 are verified and are confirmed by the simulation results.
texts.text: This section exemplifies the main results presented in Sections 3 - 5 through a series of simulation studies. In particular, we are interested in applying the EM algorithm to get maximum likelihood estimates ̂ θ 0 of the distribution parameter θ 0 of the process X (6) based on Monte Carlo simulation of its sample paths. Algorithm for simulation of the sample paths is presented. Estimated standard error ̂ Var( ̂ θ 0 ) of the MLE ̂ θ 0 is obtained using the inverse of observed Fisher information matrix J p ( ̂ θ 0 ) and compared with the actual standard errors Var( ̂ θ 0 ). Asymptotic properties (consistency, normality, and efficiency) of the MLE ̂ θ 0 are verified and are confirmed by the simulation results.

texts.orig: Using these K independent observations, the EM algorithm is applied to obtain maximum likelihood estimate ̂ θ 0 of the initial value θ 0 . To verify the consistency of the MLE ̂ θ 0 , a set of N = 200 independent sample paths of size K = 750 , 1500 , and K = 3000 are generated respectively. To each set N = 200 observations of a specified sample size K , the EM algorithm is applied to get N independent sets of MLE { ̂ θ n,K : n = 1 , · · · , N } . This process is repeated for each K . The biases N -1 ∑ N n =1 ( ̂ θ n,K -θ 0 ) and estimated standard errors (SE) of ̂ θ 0 are calculated using the inverse of information matrix J p ( ̂ θ 0 ), compared with the root of mean squared error (RMSE). Furthermore, the p-value of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic (KS) is computed for the standardized biases. The KS statistic provides a goodness-of-fit measure between the empirical distribution of the biases and the N (0 , 1) cumulative distribution function. The results are presented in Table 2 and Table 3.

The biases N -1 ∑ N n =1 ( ̂ θ n,K -θ 0 ) and estimated standard errors (SE) of ̂ θ 0 are calculated using the inverse of information matrix J p ( ̂ θ 0 ), compared with the root of mean squared error (RMSE). Furthermore, the p-value of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic (KS) is computed for the standardized biases. The KS statistic provides a goodness-of-fit measure between the empirical distribution of the biases and the N (0 , 1) cumulative distribution function. The results are presented in Table 2 and Table 3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Concluding Remarks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper proposed and estimated a new class of conditional Markov jump processes with regime switching and path dependence. It is a non-trivial generalization of the Markov process which allows the process to adjust the transition rate as it moves from one state to another based on its current state and time as well as its past trajectories through their likelihood function. The transition from current state to another state depends on the amount of time the process stays in the state. It was shown that the process has a distributional equivalent stochastic representation with a general mixture of Markov jump processes discussed in [15] and [35]. Maximum likelihood estimates (MLE) of the distribution parameters of the process are given in closed form which makes the EM computation for the estimation fast and stable. Asymptotic properties (consistency, asymptotic normality, and efficiency) of the MLE were derived. In particular, the covariance matrix of the MLE for transition rates coincides with the Cram´ er-Rao lower bound (the inverse of expected Fisher information matrix), and is less for the covariance matrix of the MLE for regime membership. These findings show that the MLE of the transition rates is asymptotically efficient , and is asymptotically superefficient for the MLE of the regime membership. See van der Vaart [38] for details on supperefficient estimator. Estimated standard errors of the MLE were presented explicitly in a simplified form of Louis' general matrix formula [24]. The results complement the recent work on maximum likelihood estimation for the general mixture of Markov jump processes [15]. A series of simulation study show that the estimation was accurate and confirmed the asymptotic properties of the MLE. For future works, we intend to estimate the (joint) probability distribution of exit times to absorbing states of the proposed conditional Markov jump process using real dataset and covariates under periodic observation of the sample paths. Its nonstationary property, ability to explicitly include past information in the distribution, and available explicit maximum likelihood estimates should offer potential for variety of applications of the model.
